mn77:1.2: at one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels' feeding ground.
mn77:10.1: There are, Udāyī, five other qualities because of which my disciples honor me, respect me, esteem me, venerate me, and dwell relying on me after honoring and respecting me.
mn77:10.3: Here, Udāyī, my disciples esteem me regarding the higher ethics:
mn77:10.4: ‘The ascetic Gotama is ethical, endowed with the highest aggregate of ethics.’
mn77:11.1: That my disciples esteem me regarding the higher ethics, Udāyī:
mn77:11.2: ‘The ascetic Gotama is ethical, endowed with the highest aggregate of ethics’ — this is the first quality because of which my disciples honor me, respect me, esteem me, venerate me, and dwell relying on me after honoring and respecting me.
mn77:12.1: Furthermore, Udāyī, my disciples esteem me regarding excellent knowledge and vision:
mn77:12.10: the ascetic Gotama teaches *dhamma* through direct knowledge, not without direct knowledge;
mn77:12.11: the ascetic Gotama teaches *dhamma* with a basis, not without a basis;
mn77:12.12: the ascetic Gotama teaches *dhamma* that has a guiding effect, not without a guiding effect’ — this is the second quality because of which my disciples honor me, respect me, esteem me, venerate me, and dwell relying on me after honoring and respecting me.
mn77:12.3: seeing, the ascetic Gotama says, “I see”;
mn77:12.4: the ascetic Gotama teaches *dhamma* through direct knowledge, not without direct knowledge;
mn77:12.5: the ascetic Gotama teaches *dhamma* with a basis, not without a basis;
mn77:12.6: the ascetic Gotama teaches *dhamma* that has a guiding effect, not without a guiding effect.’
mn77:12.7: That my disciples esteem me regarding excellent knowledge and vision, Udāyī:
mn77:12.9: seeing, the ascetic Gotama says, “I see”;
mn77:13.1: Furthermore, Udāyī, my disciples esteem me regarding higher discernment [*adhipaññāya*]:
mn77:13.10: ‘The ascetic Gotama is discerning [*paññavā*], endowed with the highest aggregate of discernment [*paññākkhandhena*];
mn77:13.11: it is impossible that he would not see some future line of argument, or would not, by means of *dhamma*, refute an opposing doctrine when it has arisen—
mn77:13.13: This is the third quality because of which my disciples honor me, respect me, esteem me, venerate me, and dwell relying on me after honoring and respecting me.
mn77:13.2: ‘The ascetic Gotama is discerning [*paññavā*], endowed with the highest aggregate of discernment [*paññākkhandhena*];
mn77:13.3: it is impossible that he would not see some future line of argument, or would not, by means of *dhamma*, thoroughly refute an opposing doctrine when it has arisen.’
mn77:13.5: When my disciples know and see in this way, would they interrupt me in the middle of a talk?”
mn77:13.7: “And I, Udāyī, do not look to my disciples for instruction;
mn77:13.8: rather, my disciples look to me for instruction.
mn77:13.9: That my disciples esteem me regarding higher discernment [*adhipaññāya*], Udāyī:
mn77:14.1: Furthermore, Udāyī, when my disciples, overcome by whatever *dukkha*, afflicted by *dukkha*, approach me and ask about the noble truth of *dukkha*, when asked about the noble truth of *dukkha* I answer them, and I satisfy their minds with the answer to the question;
mn77:14.10: I satisfy their minds with the answer to the question.
mn77:14.11: This is the fourth quality because of which my disciples honor me, respect me, esteem me, venerate me, and dwell relying on me after honoring and respecting me.
mn77:14.2: they ask me about the arising of *dukkha* …
mn77:14.4: they ask me about the noble truth of the practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha*, and when asked about the noble truth of the practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha*, I answer them, and I satisfy their minds with the answer to the question.
mn77:14.5: That my disciples, overcome by whatever *dukkha*, afflicted by *dukkha*, approach me and ask about the noble truth of *dukkha*, and when asked about the noble truth of *dukkha* I answer them, and I satisfy their minds with the answer to the question;
mn77:14.6: they ask me about the arising of *dukkha* …
mn77:14.8: they ask me about the noble truth of the practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha*.
mn77:14.9: When asked about the noble truth of the practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha*, I answer them.
mn77:15.1: Furthermore, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples develop [*bhāventi*] the four *satipaṭṭhānas*.
mn77:15.2: Here, Udāyī, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body, ardent, fully aware [*sampajāno*], with *sati* [*satimā*], having removed covetousness and displeasure for the world;
mn77:15.4: he dwells contemplating mind [*citte*] in mind [*citte*] …
mn77:15.5: he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas*, ardent, fully aware [*sampajāno*], with *sati* [*satimā*], having removed covetousness and displeasure for the world.
mn77:15.6: And there many of my disciples dwell having reached the perfection and completion of direct knowledge.
mn77:16.1: Furthermore, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples develop [*bhāventi*] the four right strivings.
mn77:16.2: Here, Udāyī, a *bhikkhu* generates desire, strives, arouses energy, uplifts the mind [*cittaṁ*], and makes effort for the non-arising of unarisen bad, unskillful *dhammas*;
mn77:16.3: he generates desire, strives, arouses energy, uplifts the mind [*cittaṁ*], and makes effort for the abandoning of arisen bad, unskillful *dhammas*;
mn77:16.4: he generates desire, strives, arouses energy, uplifts the mind [*cittaṁ*], and makes effort for the arising of unarisen skillful *dhammas*;
mn77:16.5: he generates desire, strives, arouses energy, uplifts the mind [*cittaṁ*], and makes effort for the stability, non-confusion, increase, expansion, development [*bhāvanāya*], and fulfillment of arisen skillful *dhammas*.
mn77:16.6: And there many of my disciples dwell having reached the perfection and completion of direct knowledge.
mn77:17.1: Furthermore, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples develop [*bhāventi*] the four bases of spiritual power.
mn77:17.2: Here, Udāyī, a *bhikkhu* develops [*bhāveti*] the basis of spiritual power endowed with *samādhi* due to desire and volitional efforts of striving,
mn77:17.3: develops [*bhāveti*] the basis of spiritual power endowed with *samādhi* due to energy and volitional efforts of striving,
mn77:17.4: develops [*bhāveti*] the basis of spiritual power endowed with *samādhi* due to mind [*citta*] and volitional efforts of striving,
mn77:17.5: develops [*bhāveti*] the basis of spiritual power endowed with *samādhi* due to investigation and volitional efforts of striving.
mn77:17.6: And there many of my disciples dwell having reached the perfection and completion of direct knowledge.
mn77:18.1: Furthermore, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples develop [*bhāventi*] the five *indriyas*.
mn77:18.2: Here, Udāyī, a *bhikkhu* develops [*bhāveti*] the faculty of faith, leading to stilling, leading to awakening;
mn77:18.6: develops [*bhāveti*] the faculty of discernment [*paññindriyaṁ*], leading to stilling, leading to awakening.
mn77:18.7: And there many of my disciples dwell having reached the perfection and completion of direct knowledge.
mn77:19.1: Furthermore, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples develop [*bhāventi*] the five *balas*.
mn77:19.2: Here, Udāyī, a *bhikkhu* develops [*bhāveti*] the *bala* of faith, leading to stilling, leading to awakening;
mn77:19.6: develops [*bhāveti*] the *bala* of discernment [*paññābalaṁ*], leading to stilling, leading to awakening.
mn77:19.7: And there many of my disciples dwell having reached the perfection and completion of direct knowledge.
mn77:2.1: Now at that time several well-known, famous wanderers were residing in the wanderers' park at the peacocks' feeding ground, namely—
mn77:2.2: Annabhāra, Varadhara, the wanderer Sakuludāyī, and other well-known, famous wanderers.
mn77:20.1: Furthermore, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples develop [*bhāventi*] the seven *bojjhaṅgas*.
mn77:20.2: Here, Udāyī, a *bhikkhu* develops [*bhāveti*] the *bojjhaṅga* of *sati*, dependent on seclusion [*viveka*], dependent on fading, dependent on cessation, maturing in relinquishment;
mn77:20.8: develops [*bhāveti*] the *bojjhaṅga* of equanimity [*upekkhāsambojjhaṅgaṁ*], dependent on seclusion [*viveka*], dependent on fading, dependent on cessation, maturing in relinquishment.
mn77:20.9: And there many of my disciples dwell having reached the perfection and completion of direct knowledge.
mn77:21.1: Furthermore, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples develop [*bhāventi*] the noble eightfold path.
mn77:21.2: Here, Udāyī, a *bhikkhu* develops [*bhāveti*] right view, develops [*bhāveti*] right intention, develops [*bhāveti*] right speech, develops [*bhāveti*] right action, develops [*bhāveti*] right livelihood, develops [*bhāveti*] right effort, develops [*bhāveti*] right *sati*, develops [*bhāveti*] right *samādhi*.
mn77:21.3: And there many of my disciples dwell having reached the perfection and completion of direct knowledge.
mn77:22.1: Furthermore, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples develop [*bhāventi*] the eight liberations.
mn77:22.10: With the complete transcending of the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ*], thinking ‘*viññāṇa* is infinite,’ one enters and dwells in the dimension of infinite *viññāṇa* [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*];
mn77:22.12: With the complete transcending of the dimension of infinite *viññāṇa* [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*], thinking ‘there is nothing,’ one enters and dwells in the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*];
mn77:22.14: With the complete transcending of the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*], one enters and dwells in the dimension of neither-perception-nor-non-perception [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ*];
mn77:22.16: With the complete transcending of the dimension of neither-perception-nor-non-perception [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ*], one enters and dwells in the cessation of perception [*saññā*] and what is felt [*vedayita*];
mn77:22.18: And there many of my disciples dwell having reached the perfection and completion of direct knowledge.
mn77:22.4: With no internal perception of form [*arūpasaññī*], one sees forms [*rūpāni*] externally;
mn77:22.6: One is resolved only on the beautiful;
mn77:22.8: With the complete transcending of perceptions of form [*rūpasaññānaṁ*], with the disappearance of perceptions of resistance, with non-attention to perceptions of diversity, thinking ‘space is infinite,’ one enters and dwells in the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ*];
mn77:23.1: Furthermore, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples develop [*bhāventi*] the eight dimensions of mastery [*abhibhāyatanāni*].
mn77:23.10: This is the third dimension of mastery [*abhibhāyatanaṁ*].
mn77:23.11: With no internal perception of form [*arūpasaññī*], one sees forms [*rūpāni*] externally, measureless, beautiful and ugly.
mn77:23.12: Perceiving thus, ‘Having mastered them, I know, I see.’
mn77:23.13: This is the fourth dimension of mastery [*abhibhāyatanaṁ*].
mn77:23.14: With no internal perception of form [*arūpasaññī*], one sees forms [*rūpāni*] externally that are blue, blue-colored, blue in appearance, and blue-shining.
mn77:23.15: Just as a flax flower is blue, blue-colored, blue in appearance, and blue-shining, or as a cloth from Bārāṇasī, polished on both sides, is blue, blue-colored, blue in appearance, and blue-shining;
mn77:23.16: so too, with no internal perception of form [*arūpasaññī*], one sees forms [*rūpāni*] externally that are blue, blue-colored, blue in appearance, and blue-shining.
mn77:23.17: Perceiving thus, ‘Having mastered them, I know, I see.’
mn77:23.18: This is the fifth dimension of mastery [*abhibhāyatanaṁ*].
mn77:23.19: With no internal perception of form [*arūpasaññī*], one sees forms [*rūpāni*] externally that are yellow, yellow-colored, yellow in appearance, and yellow-shining.
mn77:23.2: Internally perceiving form [*rūpasaññī*], one sees forms [*rūpāni*] externally, limited, beautiful and ugly.
mn77:23.20: Just as a kaṇikāra flower is yellow, yellow-colored, yellow in appearance, and yellow-shining, or as a cloth from Bārāṇasī, polished on both sides, is yellow, yellow-colored, yellow in appearance, and yellow-shining;
mn77:23.21: so too, with no internal perception of form [*arūpasaññī*], one sees forms [*rūpāni*] externally that are yellow, yellow-colored, yellow in appearance, and yellow-shining.
mn77:23.22: Perceiving thus, ‘Having mastered them, I know, I see.’
mn77:23.23: This is the sixth dimension of mastery [*abhibhāyatanaṁ*].
mn77:23.24: With no internal perception of form [*arūpasaññī*], one sees forms [*rūpāni*] externally that are red, red-colored, red in appearance, and red-shining.
mn77:23.25: Just as a bandhujīvaka flower is red, red-colored, red in appearance, and red-shining, or as a cloth from Bārāṇasī, polished on both sides, is red, red-colored, red in appearance, and red-shining;
mn77:23.26: so too, with no internal perception of form [*arūpasaññī*], one sees forms [*rūpāni*] externally that are red, red-colored, red in appearance, and red-shining.
mn77:23.27: Perceiving thus, ‘Having mastered them, I know, I see.’
mn77:23.28: This is the seventh dimension of mastery [*abhibhāyatanaṁ*].
mn77:23.29: With no internal perception of form [*arūpasaññī*], one sees forms [*rūpāni*] externally that are white, white-colored, white in appearance, and white-shining.
mn77:23.3: Perceiving thus, ‘Having mastered them, I know, I see.’
mn77:23.30: Just as the morning star is white, white-colored, white in appearance, and white-shining, or as a cloth from Bārāṇasī, polished on both sides, is white, white-colored, white in appearance, and white-shining;
mn77:23.31: so too, with no internal perception of form [*arūpasaññī*], one sees forms [*rūpāni*] externally that are white, white-colored, white in appearance, and white-shining.
mn77:23.32: Perceiving thus, ‘Having mastered them, I know, I see.’
mn77:23.33: This is the eighth dimension of mastery [*abhibhāyatanaṁ*].
mn77:23.34: And there many of my disciples dwell having reached the perfection and completion of direct knowledge.
mn77:23.4: This is the first dimension of mastery [*abhibhāyatanaṁ*].
mn77:23.5: Internally perceiving form [*rūpasaññī*], one sees forms [*rūpāni*] externally, measureless, beautiful and ugly.
mn77:23.6: Perceiving thus, ‘Having mastered them, I know, I see.’
mn77:23.7: This is the second dimension of mastery [*abhibhāyatanaṁ*].
mn77:23.8: With no internal perception of form [*arūpasaññī*], one sees forms [*rūpāni*] externally, limited, beautiful and ugly.
mn77:23.9: Perceiving thus, ‘Having mastered them, I know, I see.’
mn77:24.1: Furthermore, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples develop [*bhāventi*] the ten kasiṇa dimensions [*kasiṇāyatanāni*].
mn77:24.11: one perceives the *viññāṇa* kasiṇa above, below, across, non-dual and measureless.
mn77:24.12: And there many of my disciples dwell having reached the perfection and completion of direct knowledge.
mn77:24.2: One perceives the earth kasiṇa above, below, across, non-dual and measureless;
mn77:25.1: Furthermore, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples develop [*bhāventi*] the four *jhānas*.
mn77:25.2: Here, Udāyī, secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful *dhammas*, a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*, which has thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*viveka*].
mn77:25.3: He drenches, steeps, fills, and pervades this very body with the *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*viveka*], so that there is no part of his whole body unpervaded by the *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*viveka*].
mn77:25.4: Just as, Udāyī, a skilled bathman or bathman's apprentice might pour bath powder into a bronze basin and knead it, sprinkling it again and again with water, so that the ball of bath powder would be permeated, suffused, saturated, and soaked inside and out with moisture, yet would not drip;
mn77:25.5: so too, Udāyī, a *bhikkhu* drenches, steeps, fills, and pervades this very body with the *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*viveka*], so that there is no part of his whole body unpervaded by the *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*viveka*].
mn77:26.1: Furthermore, Udāyī, with the stilling of thoughts [*vitakka*] and examinations [*vicāra*], a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the second *jhāna*, with internal confidence …
mn77:26.2: He drenches, steeps, fills, and pervades this very body with the *pīti* and *sukha* born of *samādhi*, so that there is no part of his whole body unpervaded by the *pīti* and *sukha* born of *samādhi*.
mn77:26.3: Just as, Udāyī, there might be a deep lake with spring water. It would have no inlet for water from the eastern direction, no inlet for water from the western direction, no inlet for water from the northern direction, no inlet for water from the southern direction, and the sky would not from time to time pour down a proper shower;
mn77:26.4: yet a cool stream of water would well up from within that very lake, drenching, steeping, filling, and pervading that very lake with cool water, so that there would be no part of the whole lake unpervaded by cool water.
mn77:26.5: So too, Udāyī, a *bhikkhu* drenches, steeps, fills, and pervades this very body with the *pīti* and *sukha* born of *samādhi*, so that there is no part of his whole body unpervaded by the *pīti* and *sukha* born of *samādhi*.
mn77:27.1: Furthermore, Udāyī, with the fading of *pīti* … a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the third *jhāna*.
mn77:27.2: He drenches, steeps, fills, and pervades this very body with *sukha* free of *pīti*, so that there is no part of his whole body unpervaded by *sukha* free of *pīti*.
mn77:27.3: Just as, Udāyī, in a lotus pond of blue lotuses, red lotuses, or white lotuses, some blue lotuses, red lotuses, or white lotuses, born in the water, grown in the water, never rising above the water, nourished while immersed within, would be drenched, steeped, filled, and pervaded from their tips to their roots with cool water, so that there would be no part of all those blue lotuses, red lotuses, or white lotuses unpervaded by cool water;
mn77:27.4: so too, Udāyī, a *bhikkhu* drenches, steeps, fills, and pervades this very body with *sukha* free of *pīti*, so that there is no part of his whole body unpervaded by *sukha* free of *pīti*.
mn77:28.1: Furthermore, Udāyī, with the abandoning of *sukha* and the abandoning of *dukkha*, and with the earlier disappearance of gladness and distress, a *bhikkhu* enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*, which is neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha* and has purity of *sati* through equanimity [*upekkhā*].
mn77:28.2: He sits pervading this very body with a pure and bright mind [*cetasā*], so that there is no part of his whole body unpervaded by the pure and bright mind [*cetasā*].
mn77:28.3: Just as, Udāyī, a man might sit covered from head down with a white cloth, so that there would be no part of his whole body unpervaded by the white cloth;
mn77:28.4: so too, Udāyī, a *bhikkhu* sits pervading this very body with a pure and bright mind [*cetasā*], so that there is no part of his whole body unpervaded by the pure and bright mind [*cetasā*].
mn77:28.5: And there many of my disciples dwell having reached the perfection and completion of direct knowledge.
mn77:29.1: Furthermore, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples understand thus:
mn77:29.10: ‘This body of mine has form [*rūpī*], is made of the four great elements, born from mother and father, built up by boiled rice and porridge, impermanent by nature through rubbing, massaging, breaking up, and dispersal;
mn77:29.11: and this *viññāṇa* of mine is supported here, bound here.’
mn77:29.12: And there many of my disciples dwell having reached the perfection and completion of direct knowledge.
mn77:29.2: ‘This body of mine has form [*rūpī*], is made of the four great elements, born from mother and father, built up by boiled rice and porridge, impermanent by nature through rubbing, massaging, breaking up, and dispersal;
mn77:29.3: and this *viññāṇa* of mine is supported here, bound here.’
mn77:29.4: Just as, Udāyī, a beryl gem, beautiful, well-born, eight-faceted, well polished, clear, bright, and complete in every respect;
mn77:29.5: and through it a thread were strung, blue or yellow or red or white or pale;
mn77:29.6: then a man with good eyes, taking it in his hand, might examine it:
mn77:29.7: ‘This is a beryl gem, beautiful, well-born, eight-faceted, well polished, clear, bright, and complete in every respect;
mn77:29.8: and through it a thread is strung, blue or yellow or red or white or pale.’
mn77:29.9: So too, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples understand thus:
mn77:3.1: Then, in the morning, the *Bhagavā* dressed, took his bowl and robe, and entered Rājagaha for alms.
mn77:3.3: “It is still too early to walk for alms in Rājagaha.
mn77:3.4: What if I were to go to the wanderers' park at the peacocks' feeding ground, to the wanderer Sakuludāyī?”
mn77:30.1: Furthermore, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples create from this body another body, having form [*rūpiṁ*], mind-made [*manomayaṁ*], complete in all its limbs and parts, not deficient in any *indriya*.
mn77:30.10: ‘This is the snake, this is the basket; the snake is one thing, the basket another; but the snake has been drawn out from the basket.’
mn77:30.11: So too, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples create from this body another body, having form [*rūpiṁ*], mind-made [*manomayaṁ*], complete in all its limbs and parts, not deficient in any *indriya*.
mn77:30.12: And there many of my disciples dwell having reached the perfection and completion of direct knowledge.
mn77:30.2: Just as, Udāyī, a man might draw a reed out from its sheath;
mn77:30.4: ‘This is the sheath, this is the reed; the sheath is one thing, the reed another; but the reed has been drawn out from the sheath.’
mn77:30.5: Or just as, Udāyī, a man might draw a sword out from its scabbard;
mn77:30.7: ‘This is the sword, this is the scabbard; the sword is one thing, the scabbard another; but the sword has been drawn out from the scabbard.’
mn77:30.8: Or just as, Udāyī, a man might draw a snake out from a basket;
mn77:31.1: Furthermore, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples experience many kinds of spiritual power: having been one, they become many; having been many, they become one; they appear and disappear; they go unhindered through walls, ramparts, and mountains as if through space; they dive in and out of the earth as if it were water; they walk on water without breaking it as if on earth; sitting cross-legged, they travel through space like a winged bird; with their hand they touch and stroke the moon and sun, so mighty and powerful; they wield bodily mastery as far as the Brahmā world.
mn77:31.2: Just as, Udāyī, a skilled potter or potter's apprentice, with well-prepared clay, could make and produce whatever kind of vessel he wished;
mn77:31.3: or just as, Udāyī, a skilled ivory-worker or ivory-worker's apprentice, with well-prepared ivory, could make and produce whatever kind of ivory work he wished;
mn77:31.4: or just as, Udāyī, a skilled goldsmith or goldsmith's apprentice, with well-prepared gold, could make and produce whatever kind of gold work he wished.
mn77:31.5: So too, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples experience many kinds of spiritual power: having been one, they become many; having been many, they become one; they appear and disappear; they go unhindered through walls, ramparts, and mountains as if through space; they dive in and out of the earth as if it were water; they walk on water without breaking it as if on earth; sitting cross-legged, they travel through space like a winged bird; with their hand they touch and stroke the moon and sun, so mighty and powerful; they wield bodily mastery as far as the Brahmā world.
mn77:31.6: And there many of my disciples dwell having reached the perfection and completion of direct knowledge.
mn77:32.1: Furthermore, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples, with the divine ear element purified and surpassing the human, hear both kinds of sounds, divine and human, whether far or near.
mn77:32.2: Just as, Udāyī, a strong conch blower could easily make himself heard in the four directions;
mn77:32.3: so too, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples, with the divine ear element purified and surpassing the human, hear both kinds of sounds, divine and human, whether far or near.
mn77:32.4: And there many of my disciples dwell having reached the perfection and completion of direct knowledge.
mn77:33.1: Furthermore, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples, having encompassed the mind [*ceto*] of other beings, other persons, with their own mind [*cetasā*], understand it—
mn77:33.10: they understand an expansive mind [*cittaṁ*] as ‘an expansive mind [*cittaṁ*],’
mn77:33.11: and a non-expansive mind [*cittaṁ*] as ‘a non-expansive mind [*cittaṁ*]’;
mn77:33.12: they understand a surpassable mind [*cittaṁ*] as ‘a surpassable mind [*cittaṁ*],’
mn77:33.13: and an unsurpassed mind [*cittaṁ*] as ‘an unsurpassed mind [*cittaṁ*]’;
mn77:33.14: they understand a mind [*cittaṁ*] endowed with *samādhi* [*samāhitaṁ*] as ‘a mind [*cittaṁ*] endowed with *samādhi* [*samāhitaṁ*],’
mn77:33.15: and a mind [*cittaṁ*] not endowed with *samādhi* [*asamāhitaṁ*] as ‘a mind [*cittaṁ*] not endowed with *samādhi* [*asamāhitaṁ*]’;
mn77:33.16: they understand a liberated mind [*cittaṁ*] as ‘a liberated mind [*cittaṁ*],’
mn77:33.17: and an unliberated mind [*cittaṁ*] as ‘an unliberated mind [*cittaṁ*].’
mn77:33.18: Just as, Udāyī, a woman or man, young, youthful, fond of adornment, examining their own face's image [*mukhanimittaṁ*] in a clean, bright mirror or a clear bowl of water, would know if there were a blemish as ‘there is a blemish,’ or if there were no blemish as ‘there is no blemish’;
mn77:33.19: so too, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples, having encompassed the mind [*ceto*] of other beings, other persons, with their own mind [*cetasā*], understand it—
mn77:33.2: they understand a mind [*cittaṁ*] with greed as ‘a mind [*cittaṁ*] with greed,’
mn77:33.20: they understand a mind [*cittaṁ*] with greed as ‘a mind [*cittaṁ*] with greed,’
mn77:33.3: and a mind [*cittaṁ*] without greed as ‘a mind [*cittaṁ*] without greed’;
mn77:33.35: they understand an unliberated mind [*cittaṁ*] as ‘an unliberated mind [*cittaṁ*].’
mn77:33.36: And there many of my disciples dwell having reached the perfection and completion of direct knowledge.
mn77:33.4: they understand a mind [*cittaṁ*] with hate as ‘a mind [*cittaṁ*] with hate,’
mn77:33.5: and a mind [*cittaṁ*] without hate as ‘a mind [*cittaṁ*] without hate’;
mn77:33.6: they understand a mind [*cittaṁ*] with delusion as ‘a mind [*cittaṁ*] with delusion,’
mn77:33.7: and a mind [*cittaṁ*] without delusion as ‘a mind [*cittaṁ*] without delusion’;
mn77:33.8: they understand a contracted mind [*cittaṁ*] as ‘a contracted mind [*cittaṁ*],’
mn77:33.9: and a distracted mind [*cittaṁ*] as ‘a distracted mind [*cittaṁ*]’;
mn77:34.1: Furthermore, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples recollect many kinds of former dwellings, namely: one birth, two births, three births, four births, five births, ten births, twenty births, thirty births, forty births, fifty births, a hundred births, a thousand births, a hundred thousand births, many eons of contraction, many eons of expansion, many eons of contraction and expansion: ‘There I was named thus, of such a clan, of such appearance, with such food, experiencing such *sukha* and *dukkha*, with such a life-span. Passing away from there, I arose elsewhere; there too I was named thus, of such a clan, of such appearance, with such food, experiencing such *sukha* and *dukkha*, with such a life-span. Passing away from there, I arose here.’ Thus they recollect many kinds of former dwellings with their features and details.
mn77:34.2: Just as, Udāyī, a man might go from his own village to another village, and from that village to another village; then from that village he might return to his own village. It might occur to him: ‘I went from my own village to that other village; there I stood in such a way, sat in such a way, spoke in such a way, and was silent in such a way. From that village too I went to that other village; there too I stood in such a way, sat in such a way, spoke in such a way, and was silent in such a way. Then from that village I returned to my own village.’
mn77:34.3: So too, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples recollect many kinds of former dwellings, namely: one birth … thus they recollect many kinds of former dwellings with their features and details.
mn77:34.4: And there many of my disciples dwell having reached the perfection and completion of direct knowledge.
mn77:35.1: Furthermore, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples, with the divine eye purified and surpassing the human, see beings passing away and being reborn, inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, fortunate and unfortunate, and understand beings as faring according to *kamma*: ‘These dear beings were endowed with bad conduct of body, bad conduct of speech, and bad conduct of mind, revilers of the noble ones, of wrong view, undertaking *kamma* based on wrong view; with the breakup of the body, after death, they have arisen in a state of deprivation, a bad destination, the abyss, hell. But these dear beings were endowed with good conduct of body, good conduct of speech, and good conduct of mind, non-revilers of the noble ones, of right view, undertaking *kamma* based on right view; with the breakup of the body, after death, they have arisen in a good destination, a heavenly world.’ Thus, with the divine eye purified and surpassing the human, they see beings passing away and being reborn, inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, fortunate and unfortunate, and understand beings as faring according to *kamma*.
mn77:35.2: Just as, Udāyī, there were two houses with doors, and a man with good eyes, standing in the middle, might see people entering a house, leaving it, walking to and fro, and wandering about;
mn77:35.3: so too, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples, with the divine eye purified and surpassing the human, see beings passing away and being reborn, inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, fortunate and unfortunate, and understand beings as faring according to *kamma* …
mn77:35.4: and there many of my disciples dwell having reached the perfection and completion of direct knowledge.
mn77:36.1: Furthermore, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples, with the destruction of the *āsavas*, having directly known and realized for themselves in this very life the mind-liberation [*cetovimuttiṁ*] and discernment-liberation [*paññāvimuttiṁ*] that are without *āsavas*, enter and dwell in them.
mn77:36.2: Just as, Udāyī, in a mountain hollow there were a lake, clear, bright, and unclouded, and a man with good eyes standing on the bank might see shellfish and mussels, gravel and pebbles, and schools of fish moving and still. It might occur to him: ‘This lake is clear, bright, and unclouded, and there are these shellfish and mussels, gravel and pebbles, and schools of fish moving and still.’
mn77:36.3: So too, Udāyī, I have taught my disciples the path, and practicing in accordance with it my disciples, with the destruction of the *āsavas*, having directly known and realized for themselves in this very life the mind-liberation [*cetovimuttiṁ*] and discernment-liberation [*paññāvimuttiṁ*] that are without *āsavas*, enter and dwell in them.
mn77:36.4: And there many of my disciples dwell having reached the perfection and completion of direct knowledge.
mn77:37.1: This, Udāyī, is the fifth quality because of which my disciples honor me, respect me, esteem me, venerate me, and dwell relying on me after honoring and respecting me.
mn77:38.1: These, Udāyī, are the five qualities because of which my disciples honor me, respect me, esteem me, venerate me, and dwell relying on me after honoring and respecting me.”
mn77:38.3: Satisfied, the wanderer Sakuludāyī delighted in the *Bhagavā*'s words.
mn77:38.4: The Longer Discourse with Sakuludāyī is finished, the seventh.
mn77:4.1: Then the *Bhagavā* went to the wanderers' park at the peacocks' feeding ground.
mn77:4.10: Perhaps, knowing the assembly to be quiet, he may think it worth approaching.”
mn77:4.2: Now at that time the wanderer Sakuludāyī was sitting together with a large assembly of wanderers, noisy, loud, and clamorous, engaged in many kinds of animal talk, namely—
mn77:4.3: talk of kings, talk of thieves, talk of ministers, talk of armies, talk of dangers, talk of wars, talk of food, talk of drink, talk of clothes, talk of beds, talk of garlands, talk of scents, talk of relatives, talk of vehicles, talk of villages, talk of towns, talk of cities, talk of countries, talk of women, talk of heroes, talk of streets, talk of wells, talk of the departed, miscellaneous talk, tales of the world, tales of the sea, talk of being and non-being, and so on.
mn77:4.4: The wanderer Sakuludāyī saw the *Bhagavā* coming from afar.
mn77:4.5: Having seen him, he quieted his own assembly:
mn77:4.7: good sirs, do not make a noise.
mn77:4.9: that venerable one likes quiet and praises quiet.
mn77:5.1: Then the *Bhagavā* went to the wanderer Sakuludāyī.
mn77:5.10: When the wanderer Sakuludāyī was sitting to one side, the *Bhagavā* said this to him:
mn77:5.11: “For what discussion are you now seated together, Udāyī, and what conversation among you was left unfinished?”
mn77:5.2: Then the wanderer Sakuludāyī said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn77:5.5: It has been a long time, *bhante*, since the *Bhagavā* made an occasion to come here.
mn77:5.8: The *Bhagavā* sat down on the prepared seat.
mn77:5.9: The wanderer Sakuludāyī also took a certain low seat and sat down to one side.
mn77:6.1: “Let that discussion be, *bhante*, for which we are now seated together.
mn77:6.12: Nigaṇṭha Nāṭaputta is a leader of a community, a leader of a group, a teacher of a group, known, famous, founder of a sect, well regarded by many people;
mn77:6.13: he too has come to Rājagaha for the rains residence.
mn77:6.14: This ascetic Gotama too is a leader of a community, a leader of a group, a teacher of a group, known, famous, founder of a sect, well regarded by many people;
mn77:6.15: he too has come to Rājagaha for the rains residence.
mn77:6.16: Now which of these venerable ascetics and brahmins, leaders of communities, leaders of groups, teachers of groups, known, famous, founders of sects, well regarded by many people, is honored, respected, esteemed, and venerated by his disciples, and whom do the disciples dwell relying on after honoring and respecting him?’
mn77:6.18: ‘This Pūraṇa Kassapa is a leader of a community, a leader of a group, a teacher of a group, known, famous, founder of a sect, well regarded by many people;
mn77:6.19: yet he is not honored, respected, esteemed, or venerated by his disciples, nor do Pūraṇa Kassapa's disciples dwell relying on him after honoring and respecting him.
mn77:6.2: That discussion, *bhante*, will not be hard for the *Bhagavā* to hear later.
mn77:6.20: Formerly Pūraṇa Kassapa was teaching *dhamma* to an assembly of many hundreds.
mn77:6.21: There a certain disciple of Pūraṇa Kassapa made a noise:
mn77:6.22: “Good sirs, do not ask Pūraṇa Kassapa about this matter;
mn77:6.24: we know this, ask us about this matter;
mn77:6.25: we will answer this for you.”
mn77:6.26: Formerly Pūraṇa Kassapa, with arms raised, crying, could not get them to listen:
mn77:6.27: “Good sirs, be quiet; good sirs, do not make a noise.
mn77:6.28: They are not asking you, venerable sirs, they are asking us;
mn77:6.3: In former days, *bhante*, in days still earlier, among ascetics and brahmins of other sects sitting together and gathered in the debating hall, this conversation arose:
mn77:6.30: And many disciples of Pūraṇa Kassapa, after charging him with doctrine, left:
mn77:6.31: “You do not understand this *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinayaṁ*]; I understand this *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinayaṁ*]. How could you understand this *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinayaṁ*]? You are practicing wrongly; I am practicing rightly. Mine is coherent; yours is incoherent. What should have been said first you said afterward; what should have been said afterward you said first. What you had worked out has been overturned. Your doctrine has been refuted; you have been defeated. Go and free your doctrine, or untangle it if you can.”
mn77:6.32: Thus Pūraṇa Kassapa is not honored, respected, esteemed, or venerated by his disciples, nor do Pūraṇa Kassapa's disciples dwell relying on him after honoring and respecting him.
mn77:6.33: And Pūraṇa Kassapa is abused with abuse of his *dhamma*.’
mn77:6.39: Nigaṇṭha Nāṭaputta is a leader of a community, a leader of a group, a teacher of a group, known, famous, founder of a sect, well regarded by many people;
mn77:6.4: ‘It is gain indeed, sirs, for the Aṅgas and Magadhans; well gained indeed, sirs, for the Aṅgas and Magadhans.
mn77:6.40: yet he is not honored, respected, esteemed, or venerated by his disciples, nor do Nigaṇṭha Nāṭaputta's disciples dwell relying on him after honoring and respecting him.
mn77:6.41: Formerly Nigaṇṭha Nāṭaputta was teaching *dhamma* to an assembly of many hundreds.
mn77:6.42: There a certain disciple of Nigaṇṭha Nāṭaputta made a noise:
mn77:6.43: “Good sirs, do not ask Nigaṇṭha Nāṭaputta about this matter;
mn77:6.45: we know this, ask us about this matter;
mn77:6.46: we will answer this for you.”
mn77:6.47: Formerly Nigaṇṭha Nāṭaputta, with arms raised, crying, could not get them to listen:
mn77:6.48: “Good sirs, be quiet; good sirs, do not make a noise.
mn77:6.49: They are not asking you, venerable sirs, they are asking us;
mn77:6.5: For these ascetics and brahmins, leaders of communities, leaders of groups, teachers of groups, known, famous, founders of sects, well regarded by many people, have come to Rājagaha for the rains residence.
mn77:6.51: And many disciples of Nigaṇṭha Nāṭaputta, after charging him with doctrine, left:
mn77:6.52: “You do not understand this *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinayaṁ*]; I understand this *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinayaṁ*]. How could you understand this *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinayaṁ*]? You are practicing wrongly. I am practicing rightly. Mine is coherent; yours is incoherent. What should have been said first you said afterward; what should have been said afterward you said first. What you had worked out has been overturned. Your doctrine has been refuted; you have been defeated. Go and free your doctrine, or untangle it if you can.”
mn77:6.53: Thus Nigaṇṭha Nāṭaputta is not honored, respected, esteemed, or venerated by his disciples, nor do Nigaṇṭha Nāṭaputta's disciples dwell relying on him after honoring and respecting him.
mn77:6.54: And Nigaṇṭha Nāṭaputta is abused with abuse of his *dhamma*.’
mn77:6.56: ‘This ascetic Gotama too is a leader of a community, a leader of a group, a teacher of a group, known, famous, founder of a sect, well regarded by many people;
mn77:6.57: and he is honored, respected, esteemed, and venerated by his disciples, and the ascetic Gotama's disciples dwell relying on him after honoring and respecting him.
mn77:6.58: Formerly the ascetic Gotama taught *dhamma* to an assembly of many hundreds.
mn77:6.59: There a certain disciple of the ascetic Gotama coughed.
mn77:6.6: This Pūraṇa Kassapa too is a leader of a community, a leader of a group, a teacher of a group, known, famous, founder of a sect, well regarded by many people;
mn77:6.60: Then a fellow practitioner struck him with his knee:
mn77:6.61: “Let the venerable one be quiet; let the venerable one not make a noise. Our teacher, the *Bhagavā*, is teaching *dhamma*.”
mn77:6.62: At the time when the ascetic Gotama teaches *dhamma* to an assembly of many hundreds, there is then not even the sound of sneezing or coughing among the ascetic Gotama's disciples.
mn77:6.63: That great body of people attends upon him in expectation:
mn77:6.64: “Whatever *dhamma* the *Bhagavā* will speak, we will hear it.”
mn77:6.65: Just as a man at a crossroads might press pure honey, free of dregs.
mn77:6.66: A great body of people would attend upon it in expectation.
mn77:6.67: So too, at the time when the ascetic Gotama teaches *dhamma* to an assembly of many hundreds, there is then not even the sound of sneezing or coughing among the ascetic Gotama's disciples.
mn77:6.68: That great body of people attends upon him in expectation:
mn77:6.69: “Whatever *dhamma* the *Bhagavā* will speak, we will hear it.”
mn77:6.7: he too has come to Rājagaha for the rains residence.
mn77:6.70: Even those disciples of the ascetic Gotama who, having been brought together with fellow practitioners, renounce the training and return to the lower life, still speak in praise of the teacher, speak in praise of the *dhamma*, and speak in praise of the *saṅgha*. They blame only themselves, not others: “We ourselves were unlucky, we were of little merit, for after going forth in such a well-proclaimed *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinaye*], we were unable to live the complete and pure holy life for as long as life lasts.”
mn77:6.71: They live as monastery attendants or as lay followers, undertaking the five training rules.
mn77:6.72: Thus the ascetic Gotama is honored, respected, esteemed, and venerated by his disciples, and the ascetic Gotama's disciples dwell relying on him after honoring and respecting him.’”
mn77:7.1: “But how many qualities do you see in me, Udāyī, because of which my disciples honor me, respect me, esteem me, venerate me, and dwell relying on me after honoring and respecting me?”
mn77:8.1: “I see five qualities in the *Bhagavā*, *bhante*, because of which the *Bhagavā*'s disciples honor him, respect him, esteem him, venerate him, and dwell relying on him after honoring and respecting him.
mn77:8.10: That the *Bhagavā*, *bhante*, is content with any kind of lodging and praises contentment with any kind of lodging: this is the fourth quality I see in the *Bhagavā*, *bhante*, because of which the *Bhagavā*'s disciples honor him, respect him, esteem him, venerate him, and dwell relying on him after honoring and respecting him.
mn77:8.11: Furthermore, *bhante*, the *Bhagavā* is secluded, and praises seclusion.
mn77:8.12: That the *Bhagavā*, *bhante*, is secluded and praises seclusion: this is the fifth quality I see in the *Bhagavā*, *bhante*, because of which the *Bhagavā*'s disciples honor him, respect him, esteem him, venerate him, and dwell relying on him after honoring and respecting him.
mn77:8.13: These are the five qualities I see in the *Bhagavā*, *bhante*, because of which the *Bhagavā*'s disciples honor him, respect him, esteem him, venerate him, and dwell relying on him after honoring and respecting him.”
mn77:8.3: For the *Bhagavā*, *bhante*, eats little, and praises eating little.
mn77:8.4: That the *Bhagavā*, *bhante*, eats little and praises eating little: this is the first quality I see in the *Bhagavā*, *bhante*, because of which the *Bhagavā*'s disciples honor him, respect him, esteem him, venerate him, and dwell relying on him after honoring and respecting him.
mn77:8.5: Furthermore, *bhante*, the *Bhagavā* is content with any kind of robe, and praises contentment with any kind of robe.
mn77:8.6: That the *Bhagavā*, *bhante*, is content with any kind of robe and praises contentment with any kind of robe: this is the second quality I see in the *Bhagavā*, *bhante*, because of which the *Bhagavā*'s disciples honor him, respect him, esteem him, venerate him, and dwell relying on him after honoring and respecting him.
mn77:8.7: Furthermore, *bhante*, the *Bhagavā* is content with any kind of almsfood, and praises contentment with any kind of almsfood.
mn77:8.8: That the *Bhagavā*, *bhante*, is content with any kind of almsfood and praises contentment with any kind of almsfood: this is the third quality I see in the *Bhagavā*, *bhante*, because of which the *Bhagavā*'s disciples honor him, respect him, esteem him, venerate him, and dwell relying on him after honoring and respecting him.
mn77:8.9: Furthermore, *bhante*, the *Bhagavā* is content with any kind of lodging, and praises contentment with any kind of lodging.
mn77:9.1: “If my disciples honored me, respected me, esteemed me, venerated me, and dwelt relying on me after honoring and respecting me, thinking: ‘The ascetic Gotama eats little and praises eating little,’ then, Udāyī, I have disciples who eat a cupful, half a cupful, a bilva fruit's worth, or half a bilva fruit's worth.
mn77:9.10: If my disciples honored me, respected me, esteemed me, venerated me, and dwelt relying on me after honoring and respecting me, thinking: ‘The ascetic Gotama is content with any kind of lodging and praises contentment with any kind of lodging,’ then, Udāyī, I have disciples who live at the root of trees and in the open air; for eight months they do not go under a roof.
mn77:9.11: But I, Udāyī, sometimes dwell in peaked houses, plastered within and without, sheltered from the wind, with bolts fastened and windows closed.
mn77:9.12: If my disciples honored me, respected me, esteemed me, venerated me, and dwelt relying on me after honoring and respecting me, thinking: ‘The ascetic Gotama is content with any kind of lodging and praises contentment with any kind of lodging,’ then those disciples of mine, Udāyī, who live at the root of trees and in the open air, who for eight months do not go under a roof, would not honor me, respect me, esteem me, venerate me, and dwell relying on me after honoring and respecting me because of this quality.
mn77:9.13: If my disciples honored me, respected me, esteemed me, venerated me, and dwelt relying on me after honoring and respecting me, thinking: ‘The ascetic Gotama is secluded and praises seclusion,’ then, Udāyī, I have disciples who are forest dwellers, resorting to remote lodgings; they dwell having plunged into remote lodgings in forests and woodland thickets, and every half-month they come into the midst of the *saṅgha* for the recitation of the Pātimokkha.
mn77:9.14: But I, Udāyī, sometimes dwell crowded by *bhikkhus*, *bhikkhunis*, male lay followers, female lay followers, kings, royal ministers, sectarians, and disciples of sectarians.
mn77:9.15: If my disciples honored me, respected me, esteemed me, venerated me, and dwelt relying on me after honoring and respecting me, thinking: ‘The ascetic Gotama is secluded and praises seclusion,’ then those disciples of mine, Udāyī, who are forest dwellers, resorting to remote lodgings, who dwell having plunged into remote lodgings in forests and woodland thickets, and every half-month come into the midst of the *saṅgha* for the recitation of the Pātimokkha, would not honor me, respect me, esteem me, venerate me, and dwell relying on me after honoring and respecting me because of this quality.
mn77:9.16: Thus, Udāyī, my disciples do not honor me, respect me, esteem me, venerate me, and dwell relying on me after honoring and respecting me because of these five qualities.
mn77:9.2: But I, Udāyī, sometimes eat with this bowl filled level to the brim, or even more.
mn77:9.3: If my disciples honored me, respected me, esteemed me, venerated me, and dwelt relying on me after honoring and respecting me, thinking: ‘The ascetic Gotama eats little and praises eating little,’ then those disciples of mine, Udāyī, who eat a cupful, half a cupful, a bilva fruit's worth, or half a bilva fruit's worth, would not honor me, respect me, esteem me, venerate me, and dwell relying on me after honoring and respecting me because of this quality.
mn77:9.4: If my disciples honored me, respected me, esteemed me, venerated me, and dwelt relying on me after honoring and respecting me, thinking: ‘The ascetic Gotama is content with any kind of robe and praises contentment with any kind of robe,’ then, Udāyī, I have disciples who wear rag-robes and coarse robes; having picked up rags from a charnel ground, a rubbish heap, or a shop-front, they make them into an outer robe and wear it.
mn77:9.5: But I, Udāyī, sometimes wear robes given by householders, strong, rubbed smooth by knives, and soft as bottle-gourd down.
mn77:9.6: If my disciples honored me, respected me, esteemed me, venerated me, and dwelt relying on me after honoring and respecting me, thinking: ‘The ascetic Gotama is content with any kind of robe and praises contentment with any kind of robe,’ then those disciples of mine, Udāyī, who wear rag-robes and coarse robes, who pick up rags from a charnel ground, a rubbish heap, or a shop-front, make them into an outer robe, and wear it, would not honor me, respect me, esteem me, venerate me, and dwell relying on me after honoring and respecting me because of this quality.
mn77:9.7: If my disciples honored me, respected me, esteemed me, venerated me, and dwelt relying on me after honoring and respecting me, thinking: ‘The ascetic Gotama is content with any kind of almsfood and praises contentment with any kind of almsfood,’ then, Udāyī, I have disciples who live on almsfood, going from house to house, delighting in the vow of gleaning; when they have entered among the houses, even if invited to a seat, they do not consent.
mn77:9.8: But I, Udāyī, sometimes eat at invitations, rice of fine grain, with the black grains picked out, with many soups and many sauces.
mn77:9.9: If my disciples honored me, respected me, esteemed me, venerated me, and dwelt relying on me after honoring and respecting me, thinking: ‘The ascetic Gotama is content with any kind of almsfood and praises contentment with any kind of almsfood,’ then those disciples of mine, Udāyī, who live on almsfood, going from house to house, delighting in the vow of gleaning, who when they have entered among the houses, even if invited to a seat, do not consent, would not honor me, respect me, esteem me, venerate me, and dwell relying on me after honoring and respecting me because of this quality.
